text: small miracle, perhaps, that it was standing, but that made Ronin no more willing to risk Lara’s life on it . Just north lay the remains of a tin-roofed barn. It looked to have folded neatly on top of itself, now blanketed with rust and tufts of grass . He turned to look east. The wall of dust stretched for countless miles, already flowing over the hills they’d just crossed. At best, they had three or four minutes before it hit them . Ronin carried Lara around the side, between the house and barn. The rusted carcass of a tractor jutted up out of the brush that was slowly overtaking it. Normally, he’d stop to pry open the engine housing and picked it for scrap; now, he walked past it without a second glance . The house would have to do. There was no other choice. He’d keep her shielded with his body, in case of a collapse … “There!” Lara pointed and wiggled free of his arms. “I’ve seen something like that before .” She raced ahead of him when he released his hold, toward a grass-covered mound about seventy feet away from the collapsed back porch. There was a single door on the side of the mound, its metal dulled by rust and caked-on dirt. She grasped the handle and pulled, throwing her weight into it, but it didn’t budge . Lara stepped aside when Ronin caught up. He wrapped his fingers around the handle and exerted gradual force; it was immediately clear that the door hadn’t seen any recent use. He increased the pressure, knowing it was more likely to break than to turn. The wind howled and thunder rolled across the heavens, loud enough for him to feel its soundwaves vibrate over his skin. The first dust particles hit him soon after . Lara twisted away from the wind, crouched, and raised her arms to shield herself. Time was up. The needed to get inside the house and hope for the best . With a metallic groan, the mechanism gave way, and the handle swung up. Ronin tugged the door open, battling the oncoming wind. The hinges whined. The only thing darker than the sky was the entrance to the shelter . He took hold of Lara’s arm and helped her onto the concrete steps. Ronin followed directly behind, turning to close the door. The house was completely obscured by the storm. The heavy metal door slammed closed, its sound echoing in the sudden silence. Ronin switched his optics to night vision . “Oh, fuck. I hope we don’t have to go through that again,” Lara said, breath ragged. She fumbled in the darkness until she grasped his arm . He walked down the steps slowly, allowing her ample time to feel them out. “That depends on chance, and where it is we decide to go .” At the bottom, the stairs opened on a small room, about fifteen feet across from the entrance to the door on the far wall. The ceiling was only a foot over Ronin’s head. To the left, a pair of bunks hung on the wall, piled with blankets . Something shifted beneath the cloth, rustling softly in the relative silence . Ronin stepped back. Lara gasped, stumbling, as he swung his rifle into hand and pressed the stock to his shoulder . “ Ronin ?” Holding the firearm in his right hand, he used the other to guide her to the foot of the stairs, keeping himself between her and the beds. “We’re not alone .” “I should think not,” said someone from the bunk. The blankets slid to the floor as the figure sat up. A metal arm emerged, pulling the last fabric away to reveal the sleek, interconnected torso and face plates of a skinless synth . That voice — the tone, the inflection — was familiar to Ronin . “There’s not much sense in pointing your firearm at me,” the synth said, brow plates dropping. “You were repurposed for military use in May of…well, the year doesn’t really mean anything, anymore, does it? Regardless, your reaction time would nullify any harm I might try to inflict, had I any such intent .” “I…what do you mean, repurposed?” Ronin moved his left hand to the rifle’s foregrip, as though the gesture would bring clarity. Lara’s hands settled on his back, gripping his coat . The synth tilted its head, placed a hand on its thigh, and lightly drummed its fingers. “Sometimes, I forget most of us suffered memory damage. I’ve begun to contemplate, in recent years, if you aren’t better for what you’ve lost, and I disadvantaged for all I’ve retained .” “I know your voice,” Ronin said, and lowered the barrel of his weapon. “I know you .” “Our brief encounter one hundred and eighty-five years ago was hardly enough to justify you claiming to know me. Especially as it appears we’ve both changed considerably during the ensuing time .” “You’re the Prophet .” Lara inhaled sharply . “Apparently, my reputation has surpassed digital minds. That is a name I was given, but it’s never been mine. I’ve always considered it foolish for our kind to stoop to such…superstition.” The synth slowly stood up . “Guess that shit’s more suited to my kind, right?” Lara said . “My apologies. Offense was not my intention.” The synth moved to a box near the far door, limbs bending stiffly, and crouched beside what appeared to be an old lantern. “My name is Newton. After the English mathematician, of course, as I’m sure you’ve already deduced .” “Who?” Ronin and Lara asked simultaneously . Newton struck a match, the brief flare blinding Ronin’s optics. Lara flinched as gentle light filled the chamber . “Forgive me. I’d hoped things would be…closer to the status quo out there, by now. I suppose civilization still lies dormant.” Newton turned back to them, corners of its mouth falling. “Have I offended you again, miss?
